entries.shortExplanation: 먹다 means "to eat" and is practiced through polite conversation forms.
entries.explanation: 먹다 means "to eat". The first spoken form to master is 먹어요, because it is polite enough for most everyday Korean. After that, learn the verb through real communication needs: what you usually do, what you want to do, what you can do, what you cannot do, and what you have to do. The goal is not to recite forms; the goal is to say one useful sentence and then change it safely.
entries.learnerPriority: First master 먹어요. Then practice 안 먹어요 vs 못 먹어요, because that contrast appears constantly in beginner conversations.
entries.contextHint: Use this verb when talking about ordering food, explaining food restrictions, and asking what to eat together. Start with one complete sentence and then substitute the noun, place, or time phrase.

essential.meaning: The base forms you need before building longer sentences.
essential.example: Use the polite form with staff, teachers, strangers, and most adults.

conversation.meaning: Forms for time, desire, ability, and inability.
conversation.example: These answer questions like what happened, what you will do, and what you can do.

survival.meaning: Forms for rules, permission, requests, and suggestions.
survival.example: Use request forms only when it is natural to ask someone else to do that action.

entries.nuance: 먹다 is used for meals and food. For medicine, Korean often uses 약을 먹어요, literally “eat medicine,” which means “take medicine.”
commonMistakes: Do not use the dictionary form 먹다 as a complete polite sentence.
Use 먹어요 as the default until you know the relationship is casual.

lesson.canDo: Use 먹어요 in a real beginner sentence, then change it into want, can, cannot, and have-to forms without losing the basic meaning.
studyFlow: Say 먹어요 first. This is the safest spoken form for most beginner situations.
Add one useful frame: 점심을 먹어요. Read the Hangul first, then check the romanization.
Practice the contrast between 안 먹어요 and 못 먹어요.
Open one linked pattern and come back to this card to see the same grammar in a verb form.
lesson.teacherNote: Do not ask a beginner to memorize all 15 forms at once. Teach 먹어요 first, then add one communicative need: desire (먹고 싶어요), possibility (먹을 수 있어요), or limitation (못 먹어요).
lesson.pronunciationTip: 먹다 is written meokda. In real sentences, compare the audio with the romanization because Korean sound changes can cross syllable blocks.

beginnerPath.title: 1. Memorize the safe sentence first
beginnerPath.body: 먹어요 is the everyday polite form. A beginner can use it with staff, teachers, adult classmates, and strangers. Do not start from 먹다 when speaking.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Add one real-life noun or place
beginnerPath.body: Practice the verb inside a useful frame like 점심을 먹어요. Korean words become usable only when you know what particle or place phrase usually sits beside them.

beginnerPath.title: 3. Separate choice from inability
beginnerPath.body: 안 먹어요 means you do not do it or are not doing it. 못 먹어요 means you cannot do it because of time, ability, rules, health, or circumstances.

beginnerPath.title: 4. Recycle the same verb through patterns
beginnerPath.body: Once 먹어요 feels easy, practice 먹고 싶어요, 먹을 수 있어요, and 먹어야 해요. This is how one verb becomes many real sentences.

classroomScript: Teacher says: 먹어요. Learner repeats three times.
Teacher asks a yes/no question with 점심을 먹어요. Learner answers with 네 or 아니요.
Learner changes the sentence to 안 먹어요, then 못 먹어요.
Learner says one personal sentence using today, tomorrow, here, or together.

drills.note: Change the polite present into a simple negative.
Change the verb into a desire sentence.
Use this for inability or a real limitation.

selfCheck: Can you say “I eat” politely?
Can you say “I want to eat”?
Can you explain the difference between 안 먹어요 and 못 먹어요?

entries.shortExplanation: 예약하다 means "to reserve / book" and is practiced through polite conversation forms.
entries.explanation: 예약하다 means "to reserve / book". The first spoken form to master is 예약해요, because it is polite enough for most everyday Korean. After that, learn the verb through real communication needs: what you usually do, what you want to do, what you can do, what you cannot do, and what you have to do. beginnerPath.title: 1. Memorize the safe sentence first
beginnerPath.body: 예약해요 is the everyday polite form. A beginner can use it with staff, teachers, adult classmates, and strangers. Do not start from 예약하다 when speaking.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Add one real-life noun or place
beginnerPath.body: Practice the verb inside a useful frame like 방을 예약해요. Korean words become usable only when you know what particle or place phrase usually sits beside them.

beginnerPath.title: 3. Separate choice from inability
beginnerPath.body: 예약 안 해요 means you do not do it or are not doing it. 예약 못 해요 means you cannot do it because of time, ability, rules, health, or circumstances.

beginnerPath.title: 4. Recycle the same verb through patterns

This card focuses on practical beginner use: what to say, where it fits, and what mistake to avoid.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize 좋아요. Then read the examples and notice the particle or ending attached near 좋다.
entries.contextHint: Use this word when talking about basic everyday communication. The examples show whether it naturally works with 은/는, 이/가, 을/를, 에, 에서, 로/으로, or a descriptive ending.

usagePhrases.en: It is good / I like it.

entries.nuance: 좋아요 can mean “it is good” or “I like it.” The subject often takes 이/가.
commonMistakes: Do not memorize this only as an English translation; memorize one Korean sentence frame.
For liking something, Korean often says X이/가 좋아요, not X을/를 좋아요.
notes: 좋다 is an adjective, so it does not use want/request forms like verbs.

lesson.canDo: Use 좋다 inside a real beginner sentence, not only as an isolated translation.
studyFlow: Say the word first: 좋다. Check the romanization only after trying the Hangul.
Memorize one useful chunk: 좋아요.
Notice which particle, place marker, or ending appears around the word.
Make one personal sentence by changing only one noun, place, time, or adjective.
lesson.teacherNote: 좋다 should be taught as a sentence ingredient. The student needs the common frame more than a dictionary definition.
lesson.pronunciationTip: 좋다 is written jotda. Use audio and Hangul together because romanization is only a helper.

beginnerPath.body: Use this word when talking about basic everyday communication. The examples show whether it naturally works with 은/는, 이/가, 을/를, 에, 에서, 로/으로, or a descriptive ending.
